sentences.base_language: Good evening! Do you have a reservation?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Bonsoir** = good evening — used from roughly late afternoon/early evening onwards (typically from ~6pm). Before that, **bonjour** is used all day.
**Vous avez** + noun? = do you have? — question by intonation, very common in spoken French.

sentences.base_language: Yes, a table for two, under the name Dupont.
sentences.target_language: Oui, une table pour deux personnes, au nom de Dupont.
sentences.grammarNote:
**au nom de** = under the name of — **au** = à + le.
**pour deux personnes** = for two people — **personnes** is the precise word for counting people: deux personnes, trois personnes (not *deux gens*; **les gens** is uncountable).
**Dupont** is a very common French surname, often used as a placeholder name.

**Par ici** = this way / right this way — **par** indicates direction or route: par ici (this way), par là (that way), par où ? (which way?).
**s'il vous plaît** = please (formal) — the standard polite marker in professional/service contexts.
Compare: **s'il te plaît** (tu form, informal) used with friends and family.
'La carte' = the full menu with all options. 'Le menu' in France is typically a fixed-price set meal. Always ask for 'la carte' if you want to choose freely.

**Qu'est-ce que vous recommandez ?** — **qu'est-ce que** introduces a question where the unknown is the object of the verb.
Structure: Qu'est-ce que + subject + verb: **Qu'est-ce que** vous **recommandez** ?
More formal inversion: **Que recommandez-vous ?** — grammatically correct but quite stiff in a restaurant.

sentences.base_language: The duck confit is our specialty.
sentences.target_language: Le confit de canard est notre spécialité.
sentences.grammarNote:
**notre spécialité** = our specialty — **notre** is the possessive adjective (our, singular noun): notre spécialité, notre restaurant. For plural: **nos** plats, nos vins.
**le confit de canard** = duck confit — a classic French dish of duck legs slow-cooked and preserved in their own fat, originating in southwest France.

sentences.base_language: I'll have the duck confit and a glass of red wine.
sentences.target_language: Je vais prendre le confit de canard et un verre de vin rouge.
sentences.grammarNote:
**je vais prendre** = I'll have/take — **futur proche** (near future): **aller** + infinitive. Very natural for ordering: je vais prendre, je vais manger.
**un verre de vin rouge** — **de** (not **du**) after a container noun: un verre **de** vin, une bouteille **de** rosé.
**le/la** before dish names: **le** confit de canard, **le** saumon.

sentences.base_language: And for me, the salmon please.
sentences.target_language: Et pour moi, le saumon, s'il vous plaît.
sentences.grammarNote:
**pour moi** = for me — **moi** is the disjunctive/stressed pronoun used after prepositions: pour moi, avec toi, chez lui.
**le saumon** = the salmon — definite article used when referring to a specific dish on the menu.
Note: the verb is omitted in this short response — very natural in French ordering: 'Et pour moi, le saumon' (understood: je vais prendre le saumon).

**L'addition** = the bill — elision: **l'addition** (not *la addition*).
This is the standard way to ask for the bill in a French restaurant. Just saying **l'addition !** while catching the waiter's attention is also perfectly natural.
**la note** is used in hotels; **l'addition** is specific to restaurants and cafés.

**Tout était délicieux** — **tout** (everything, masculine singular) + imperfect tense **était** (was, from **être**).
The **imparfait** is used here to describe the overall past experience as a state: it was delicious throughout the meal.
**délicieux** agrees with **tout** (masculine singular) — no change needed.
